perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall keep a written record of th e Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials furnished to it and the location of such Confidential Information and Confidential Materials. At any time, at the Disclosing Party’s request, all copies of th e Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials shall be returned to the Disclosing Party within five (5) business days of such request; provided, however, that the R eceiving Party shall be permitted to retain a list that contains general descriptions of the docum ents it has returned to the Disclosing Party to facilitate the resolution of any controversies after the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials have been returned.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party should endeavor to keep a record of the Confidential Information and Confidential Materials. Upon request, the Receiving Party will make a reasonable attempt to return the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials within a timeframe that is considered appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'shall keep' to 'should endeavor to keep' introduces ambiguity, weakening the obligation to maintain a written record. Additionally, 'reasonable attempt' and 'timeframe that is considered appropriate' create vagueness regarding the return of confidential materials, making the obligation less enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 812.081(1)(a) addresses the theft, embezzlement, or unlawful taking of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to keep records and promptly return confidential information, the modified text could impede the Disclosing Party's ability to prove misappropriation, as it creates uncertainty about whether the Receiving Party adequately protected the trade secrets.

F.S. 812.081 812.081 Theft of or trafficking in trade secrets; definitions; penalties; providing to foreign entities; restitution. — (1) As used in this section, the term: (a) “Endeavor” means to attempt or to try. (b) “Foreign agent” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government. (c) “Foreign instrumentality” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government. (d) “Obtain or use” has the same meaning as provided in s. 812.012 (3). (e) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (f) “Trade secret” means the whole or any portion or phase of any formula, pattern, device, combination of devices, or compilation of information which is for use, or is used, in the operation of a business and which provides the business an advantage, or an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over those who do not know or use it. The term includes any scientific, technical, or commercial information, including financial information, and includes any design, process, procedure, list of suppliers, list of customers, business code, or improvement thereof, whether tangible or intangible, and regardless of whether or how it is stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing. Irrespective of novelty, invention, patentability, the state of the prior art, and the level of skill in the business, art, or field to which the subject matter pertains, a trade secret is considered to be: 1. Secret; 2. Of value; 3. For use or in use by the business; and 4. Of advantage to the business, or providing an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates specific actions ('shall keep,' 'shall be returned'), while the changed version introduces ambiguity and discretion ('should endeavor to keep,' 'reasonable attempt,' 'timeframe that is considered appropriate'). This shift weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets, as outlined in Florida Statute § 812.081, especially considering that subsection (1)(a) defines 'endeavor' as merely 'to attempt or to try.' The original text provides a clear obligation, but the modified text weakens it enough to create a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party also agrees that it will, to the extent authorized by law, reasonably cooperate with the Disclosing Party in opposing th e production of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Materials; provide d, however, in the event the Receiv ing Party incurs any cost or expense in providing such cooperation, the Disc losing Party shall pr omptly reimburse the Receiving Party for any such cost or expense upon the Receiving Party’s tendering of a receipt therefor.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will consider cooperating with the Disclosing Party in opposing the production of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Materials as the Receiving Party deems fit; however, any cost or expense reimbursement will be at the sole discretion of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'agrees that it will...reasonably cooperate' to 'will consider cooperating...as the Receiving Party deems fit' makes cooperation discretionary, undermining the legal obligation. Additionally, changing 'shall promptly reimburse' to 'will be at the sole discretion of the Disclosing Party' eliminates the obligation to reimburse costs.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Rules of Civil Procedure 1.380(e) concerns sanctions for failing to cooperate in discovery. While not directly a breach, the ambiguous wording reducing cooperation to a discretionary act and eliminating mandatory reimbursement could lead to disputes over the extent of necessary cooperation, and make it harder to enforce compliance with discovery obligations. This could potentially hinder the Disclosing Party's ability to obtain necessary protections for confidential information in litigation.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language imposes a reasonable obligation on the Receiving Party to cooperate with the Disclosing Party. The changed language makes cooperation discretionary ('will consider cooperating...as the Receiving Party deems fit'), effectively removing the obligation. The change from 'shall promptly reimburse' to 'will be at the sole discretion of the Disclosing Party' eliminates the obligation to reimburse costs, which is a legal contradiction based on the definition provided for Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation, where an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law. The law explanation supports this assessment, noting that the changes could lead to disputes and hinder the Disclosing Party's ability to protect confidential information. While there are no law snippets, the Perturbation Explanation indicates that the 'legal obligation' has been undermined.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall notify the Disclosing Party promptly upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Materials, or any other breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or any of its Repr esentatives, and shall fu lly cooperate with the Disclosing Party to help the Disclosing Party rega in possession of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Materials and prevent the unauthori zed use of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Materials.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will use reasonable efforts to notify the Disclosing Party upon becoming generally aware of a potential unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Materials, or a possible other breach of this Agreement. The Receiving Party will consider assisting the Disclosing Party as it deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall notify...promptly upon discovery' to 'will use reasonable efforts to notify...upon becoming generally aware' weakens the obligation to immediately report breaches. Changing 'shall fully cooperate' to 'will consider assisting as it deems appropriate' introduces discretion, making cooperation optional.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 815.04(3)(a), part of the Florida Computer Crimes Act, addresses offenses against intellectual property. The original text clearly requires prompt notification of unauthorized use or disclosure of confidential information. The modified text weakens this obligation. Delaying or failing to report such incidents fully hinders the ability to promptly investigate and mitigate potential damages from unauthorized access or disclosure, as contemplated by the law.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall notify promptly upon discovery' to 'will use reasonable efforts to notify upon becoming generally aware' weakens the obligation to report breaches immediately. Furthermore, changing 'shall fully cooperate' to 'will consider assisting as it deems appropriate' makes cooperation optional. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this introduces discretion. The Law Explanation states that this hinders the ability to mitigate potential damages from unauthorized access, which is a purpose implied by the law. Since the scraped snippets are empty, the decision is based upon the other elements provided.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties irrevocably and uncond itionally consent to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the State of Florida and of the Un ited States of America located in the State of Florida, County of Hillsborough, for any ac tions, suits or proceedings arising out of or related to this Agreement. The Parties shall not commence any action, suit or proceeding relating to this Agreement except in such courts.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties may consider submitting to the jurisdiction of courts in Florida, if they so choose. The Parties will attempt to avoid commencing actions relating to this Agreement outside of Florida courts.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'irrevocably and unconditionally consent to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction' to 'may consider submitting to the jurisdiction' makes jurisdiction optional. Changing 'shall not commence any action...except in such courts' to 'will attempt to avoid commencing actions' weakens the restriction on commencing actions elsewhere.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 47.025 addresses contractual provisions fixing venue. While not directly nullifying the clause, the modified text introduces significant ambiguity, undermining the enforceability of the forum selection clause. The statute relies on clear and unambiguous contractual language to determine venue. The changes make it difficult to enforce the intended venue, potentially leading to litigation outside of Florida, which the original clause sought to prevent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Florida Statutes § 47.025 (2023) - Actions Against Contractors. :: 2023 Florida Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Florida Stat. 2023 Florida Stat. (here) 2022 Florida Stat. View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Florida Statutes Title VI - Civil Practice and Procedure Chapter 47 - Venue 47.025 - Actions Against Contractors. Universal Citation: FL Stat § 47.025 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 47.025 Actions against contractors.—Any venue provision in a contract for improvement to real property which requires legal action involving a resident contractor, subcontractor, sub-subcontractor, or materialman, as defined in 1 part I of chapter 713, to be brought outside this state is void as a matter of public policy. To the extent that the venue provision in the contract is void under this section, any legal action arising out of that contract shall be brought only in this state in the county where the defendant resides, where the cause of action accrued, or where the property in litigation is located, unless, after the dispute arises, the parties stipulate to another venue. History.—s. 9, ch. 99-225; s. 1, ch. 99-386. 1 Note.—As created by s. 9, ch. 99-225. Section 47.025 was also created by s. 1, ch. 99-386, and that version cites to s. 713.01. perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates exclusive jurisdiction in Florida courts, while the changed text makes jurisdiction optional and weakens restrictions on commencing actions elsewhere. The Law Explanation supports that this introduces ambiguity and undermines the enforceability of the forum selection clause. While the scraped snippet relates to actions against contractors and doesn't directly address forum selection clauses in general, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight that the changes weaken and make optional what was previously mandatory, creating a legal contradiction under the Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation definition.
